content.full_text:
Re
Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcES CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 80"7 c 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
luly 25,2002
Adolfo E. Miralles, FAIA
Miralles & Wu, LLP
729 W. Woodbury Road
Altadena, CA 91001
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-182
Dear Mr. Miralles:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the'Act").' Because your question is general in nature, we are
providing you with informal assistance.2
QUESTION
To what extent may you represent a developer in your capacity as an architect
before the west Altadena Project Area Committee, of which you are a member?
CONCLUSION
You may prepare and submit to the necessary offices or agencies drawings or
submissions of an architectural nature on behalf the developer. However, your contact
with agency staff must be limited to responding to staff questions and, if necessary,
obtaining clarification of staff requests for revisionr, *d.orn-,rrricating with staff
regarding the movement of drawings and submissions through the approval process. You
may not appear before your own committee in representing a client's interests.
' Govetnment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-02-182
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are an architect by profession and are also a member of the West Altadena
Project Area Committee ("WAPAC') in the redevelopment area where your office is
located. You have been requested by a developer to be part of their team as apaid
architectural consultant. That team will be proposing a project within the redevelopment
area.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their financial interests.
(Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from
"making," "participating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to
"influence" a governmental decision in which the ofEcial has a financial interest. The
Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether an official
has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700 (bxl) - (S).)3
The first step is used to determine if you are a public official. Under the Act, a
"public official" is defined, in part, as a "member, officer, employee or consultant of a
state or local government agency." (Section 82048.) As a member of WAPAC, you are
a public of,ficial and subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (In re
Rotman (1987) 10 FPPC Ops. 1.)
The second step of the analysis is used to determine if you are "making,"
"participating in making," or "influencing" a governmental decision. If the determination
is made that you are not engagtng in any of these actions, then the conflict-of-interest
rules are not implicated and our analysis ends.
Making a Govemmental Decision
Under the analysis prescribed by step two, a public official "makes a
goverlmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office
or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency
to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Section 87100; regulation 18702.1(a).) Based on the facts you have provided, it
appears that the proposed project will be submitted to WAPAC for approval. Therefore,
you would "make" a decision on these projects as a member of the committee if you
participate in the vote.a
3 These steps are explained in the FPPC document entitled, "CanlVote? Conflicts of Interest
Overview," which is enclosed for your information.
n Yor. question concerns only appearing before WAPAC, you have not asked about voting.
Generally, you are banned from voting on your clients'projects. If you would like to be specifically
advised with regard to voting on the matter, contact us for further advice.


File No. I-02-182
Page No. 3
Influencing a Govemmental Decision
Even if you do not partictpate in the vote before WAPAC, there are still two rules
to consider as to whether a public official uses or attempts to use his or her official
position to influence a govemmental decision. The first rule applies when the relevant
governmental decision is within or before the public official's own agency, or an agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency.
(Regulation 18702.3(a).) Regulation 18702.3(a) goes on to state:
"... the official is atternpting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influenco, ffiy member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer."
Under this rule, attempts to influence include appeaxances or contacts on behalf of
a client. However, the Act would not prohibit an official's employee orpartner from
representing a client before the official's agency even where the employee or partner uses
letterhead that includes the official's name. (NovakAdvice Letter, No. I-97-365.)
In addition, the Commission has recognized an exception for presentation of
architectural, engineering, or similar drawings by the official to his or her own agency.
As a professional architect, you would fall within this exception. Pursuant to regulation
r8702.a@):
"(b) Notwithstanding Title2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18702.3(a), an official is not
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision of an agency covered by that
subsection if the official:
'T... 1T
"(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be used by a
client in connection with a proceeding before any ageficy.
However, this provision applies only if the official has no
other direct oral or written contact with the agency with
regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except
for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions
prepared by the official."


File No. I-02-182
Page No. 4
This exception is limited to the preparation of technical documents. Please note
that the preparation of non-technical documents or other materials would not fall within
this exception. (Levinger Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.) Moreover, the exception does
not permit an official to have any other direct contact with the agency regarding the
client's project except for necessary contact with agency staff conceming the processing
or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official. 'Necessary
contact" has been narrowly construed to allow an official only to respond to questions
from agency staff regarding the evaluation of drawings and submissions prepared by the
official or relating to their movement through the approval process. (Thomson Advice
Letter, No. I-00-239 and Holbert Advice Letter, No. I-90-080.) Finally, the exception
would not allow an official to contact agency staff with respect to any drawing or
submission prepared by someone other than the official. (Smith Advice Letter,No. A-96-
022.)
The basis for this exception was set forth in the staffmemorandum submitted to
the Commission to explain the exception. The memorandum stated:
"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on
the professional activities of planning commissioners or
city councilmembers who are architects and engineers.
There was general agreement that planning
commissioners/architects should not actually represent
clients before the planning commission. It was also agreed
that such architects and engineers should be able to prepare
the drawings and plans for clients, even though the
drawings and plans would have to be submitted to the
planning commission. There seemed to be general
consensus among the city attomeys that this exception is
necessary in order to allow the local architects and
engineers to continue to practice their profession when they
act as planning commissioners or councilmernbers." (Staff
Memorandum to the Commission, dated Apil29,1935.)
Under the facts that you have presented, you may respond to staff questions and,
if necessary, obtain clarification of staff requests for revisions, and you may
communicate with staff regarding the movement of drawings and submissions through
the approval process. (Grocott Advice Letter, No. A-02-028.) However, you-may not
appear before WAPAC in representation of a client's interests. While you may attend a
WAPAC meeting at which one of your client's projects is being considered, you may not
in any way assist in the presentation of the project to WAPAC by providing information
or advice to the presenter. (Buchert Advice Letter, No.l-99-242.)s
5 While you have not asked about it, you should note that a different rule applies for appearances
before an agency other than your own. (Regulation 18702.3(b), enclosed.)


File No. I-02-182
Page No. 5
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Galena West
Counsel, Le gal Division
Enclosures
Gwje
